title: 第 6 節
en: It would be out of place to discuss at present all the arguments by which the historical origin of the Buddhistic religion has been fixed chronologically in the works here mentioned. The date of Buddha's religion : " La population de la terre est evaluee par M. Hassel a 921 millions; par Malte-Brun, a 642 millions; par d'autres, a 737 millions d'habitants. Le Buddhisme est professe dans presque tout l'empire de la Chine, qui seul, d'apres differents computs, contient de 184 a 300 millions d'habitants. Ajoutons-y les Buddhistes de plusieurs iles de l'Est, de la Cochinchine, du Siam, du pays des Birmans, del'Inde, du Nepal, du Tibet, et de la majeure partie de la Tartarie, etc., et Ton trouvera que je n'exagere pas trop le nombre des Buddhistes actuels."
1 See J. Bird, Historical Researches on the Origin and Principles of the Buddha and Jaina Religion. Bombay, 1847.

RELATION OF BUDDHISM TO ERAIIMANISM. 35 death, in the middle of the sixth century b. c, and the beginning of the Ceylonese era, 543 b. c, will have to be considered hereafter. For the present it will be sufficient to keep in mind that the Buddhistic era divides the whole history of India into two parts, in the same manner as the Christian era divides the history of the world. It is therefore of the greatest importance, with regard to the history of Yedic literature. The rise of a new religion so hostile to the hierarchical system of the Brahmans is most likely to have produced a visible effect on their sacred and theological writings. If traces of this kind can be discovered in the ancient literature of India, an important point will be gained, and it will be possible perhaps to restore to this vast mass of Brahmanic lore a certain historical connection. After the rise of a new religious doctrine in the first centuries after Buddha, it could not be expected that the Brahmanic literature should cease at once. On the contrary, we should expect at first a powerful reaction and a last effort to counteract the influence of the rising doctrine. And, as in India the religion of Buddha addressed itself more especially to the lower classes of the people, and found its strongest support amongst those who had to suffer from the exclusiveness of the Brahmanic system, a period of transition would most likely be marked by a more popular style of literature, — by an attempt to simplify the old complicated system of the Brahmanic ceremonial, till at last the political ascendency, secured to the new doctrine through its adoption by the reigning princes, like Asoka, would cause this effort also to slacken.
Before it can be shown, however, that this really D 2 36 NON-VEDIC WORKS.

took place in India, and that traces of this religious crisis exist in the Yedic literature of the Brahmans, it seems necessary to point out what Sanskrit works can be included within that literature, and what other books are to be excluded altogether when we look for evidence with regard to the true history of the Yedic age.

Let us begin by the negative process, and endeavour to separate and reject those works which do not belong to the genuine Yedic cycle. If we examine the two epic poems of India, the Ramayana and Mahabharata, we shall find it impossible to use them as authorities for the Yedic age, because we are not yet able to decide critically which parts of these poems are ancient, and which are modern and postBuddhistic, or at least retouched by the hands of late compilers and editors. There are certainly very ancient traditions and really Yedic legends in both of these poems. Some of their heroes are taken from the same epic cycle in which the Yedic poetry moves. These, however, only form subjects for episodes in the two poems, while their principal heroes are essentially different in their character and manners. In fact, though there are remains of the Yedic age to be found in the epic poems, like the stories of Urvasi and Pururavas, of Sakuntala and Dushmanta, of Uddalaka, Sunahsepha, Janaka Yaideha, and particularly of the Yedic Rishis, like Yasishtha, Yisvamitra, Yajnavalkya, Dirghatamas, Kakshivat, Kavasha, and many others, yet this would only prove that the traditions of the Yedic age were still in the mouth of the people at the time when the epic poetry of the Hindus was first composed, or that they were not yet forgotten in after times, when the Brahmans EPIC POEMS. 37 began to collect all the remains of epic songs into one large body, called the Mahabharata. If we compare the same legends as exhibited in the hymns and Brahmanas of the Veda, and as related in the Mahabharata, Ramayana, or the Puranas, the Yedic version of them will mostly be found to be more simple, more primitive, and more intelligible than those of the epic and pauranic poems. This is not meant as a denial, that real epic poetry, that is to say, a mass of popular songs, celebrating the power and exploits of gods and heroes, existed at a very early period in India, as well as among the other Aryan nations ; but it shows, that, if yet existing, it is not in the Mahabharata and Ramayana we have to look for these old songs, but rather in the Yeda itself. In the collection of the Yedic hymns, there are some which may be called epic, and may be compared with the short hymns ascribed to Homer. In the Brahmanas passages occur, in prose and verse, celebrating the actions of old kings.
The following extract from the Sankhayana-sutras (xvi. 1.), throws some light on the literature which the Brahmans possessed, in addition to what we are accustomed to call the Yeda * : —

" At the Horse-sacrifice, the Adhvaryu calls upon singers who sing to the lute (vinaganaginas), and invites them to celebrate the king, who then performs the sacrifice, together with other virtuous kings of old. On the first day of the sacrifice, the priest tells the story which begins with Manu Vaivasvata. As the people of Manu were men, and there are men present at the sacrifice, the priest teaches these, the 1 The same account is given in the Asvalayana-sutras, x. 7, and in the 6atapatha-brahmana, xiii. 3, 1, 1.
householders, by telling this story. He then says, 1 The Richverses are the Veda, this is the Veda/ and recites a hymn.

" On the second day he tells the story which begins with Yama Vaivasvata (from the iSatapatha). As the people of Yama were the fathers, and there are fathers present, he teaches the elders by this story. He then says, ' The Yajurveda is the Veda ; this is the Veda/ and recites an Anuvaka (asvamedhika) of the Yajush.

" On the third day he tells the story which begins with Varuna Aditya. As the people of Varuna were the Gandharvas, and as they are present, he teaches the young and fair youths by this story. He then says, ' The Atharva-veda is the Veda ; this is the Veda,' and recites the Bhishaja1, a work on medicine.

" On the fourth day he tells the story which begins with Soma Vaishnava (from the Satapatha). As the people of Soma were the Apsaras, and as these are present, he teaches the young and fair maids by this story. He then says, ' The Angirasa-veda is the Veda; this is the Veda,' and recites the Ghora2, another work of the Atharvanikas.

He then says, ' The Sarpavidya is the Yeda ; this is the Yeda,' and recites the Sarpavidya.1 " On the sixth day he tells the story which begins with Kuvera VaisravancC. story. He then says, * The Itihasa-veda is the Veda this is the Veda/ and recites an Itihasa.1 " On the ninth day he tells the story which begins with Tdrkshya Vaipasyata.2 As his men were the birds, and as these are present, he teaches the birds, or the young students (brahmacharin)3, by this story. He then says, ' The Purana-veda is the Veda, this is the Veda/ and recites the Purana.4 " On the tenth day he tells the story which begins with Dharma Indra (from the Satapatha). As his men were the gods, and as these are present, he teaches the young, learned, and poor priests by this story.5 He then says, ' The Samaveda is the Veda, this is the Veda/ and sings the Sama.6"
This extract shows that epic poetry, traditional as well as improvised on the spur of the moment, existed during the Vedic age.

In several parts of the Brahmanas and Aranyakas, when an account is given of the literature, known to the ancient Hindus, we meet with the names of Gatha, Narasansi, Itihasa, and Akhyana 7 (songs, legends, epic poems, and stories) as parts 2 Vaipaschita, according to Asvalayana.

EPIC TOEMS. 41 of the Yedic literature. The occurrence of titles of literary works like these, has been made use of to prove the existence, at that early period, of the writings which afterwards were designated by the same names. But though the Mahabharata is called an Itihasa, and the Ramayana an Akhyana, and though many works have in later times become famous under the name of Puranas, yet these enumerations of literary works in the Brahmanas do not refer to them.1 They contain only general names or WT«Trf% ^TT*sM I lU^ ll ^id. iv. 1, 2., iv. 5. 9.; 6atap. Brahm.

xi. 7. 1.; Atharv. Sanhita, xv. 6.: ^f^T^nf ^TTW "^ TTSTT^f TnC^I^t'^' II Cf. Aufrecht, Indische Studien, p. 133. Sayana himself is sometimes doubtful, and in his Commentary on the Taittiriya-aranyaka, for instance, he says that, by purana might be meant the Brahmanda, &c. ; and by itihasa, the Mahabharata. This, however, is a mistake, and it would bring Sayana into contradiction with himself. He has fully proved in his Introduction to the Rig-veda that in this passage of the Taittiriyaranyaka, no works separate from the Veda could be understood. Cf. Rig-veda sanhita, p. 23. Dr. Weber, in his extracts from Panini (iv. 2. 60.), shows that vyakhyana, akhyana, katha, akhyayika, itihasa, and purana, were titles of literary works known at the time of Katyayana. But he inclines to the opinion that Katyayana did not mean the Mahabharata, Ramayana, and the Puranas, as we now possess them, by these general names. Cf. Indische Studien, i. p. 147.

1 In the later literature also, names like Itihasa, Akhyana, and Purana are by no means restricted to the Mahabharata, Ramayana, and the Puranas. The Mahabharata is called Purana, Akhyana, and Itihasa. Cf. M. Bh. i. 17 — 19. Vyasa himself calls his poem, the Mahabharata, a Kavya ; and Brahma sanctions this as its proper title. Cf. M. Bh. i. 72. This passage modifies Professor Lassen's opinion as to Kavya being the distinctive title of the Ramayana. Cf. Indian Antiquities, i. 48o. The Mahabharata 42 epic toems.

titles, which have been applied to certain parts of the sacred literature, containing either stories of gods or men, or cosmogonic traditions.1 There is no allusion to any of the titles of the Puranas or to the Ramayana in Yedic works, whether Brahmanas or Sutras. But as in the Sutras of Asvalayana2 the name of the is also called the fifth Veda, or the Karshna-veda ; that is, the Veda composed by Krishna Dvaipayana Vyasa. Cf. M. Bh. i. 2300. Burnouf, Bhag. in. pref. xxi. Lassen, Ind. Antiq. I. 789.
1 Cf. Sayana, Introduction to the Rig-vedasanhita, p. 23.

2 Grihy a- Sutras, iii. 4. MS. 1978, E. I. H., reads, *TR7T- ^T^T^r: instead of ^TT^TWTTrfWr^T^r: the reading adopted by Dr. Roth (Zur Literatur, p. 27). Unfortunately the Commentary to this passage is very scanty, which is so much the more to be regretted, as the text itself seems to contain spurious additions. According to the MSS. the passage reads, ^ro ^ra: ipTf^r TTSTW *T<^f>* f^TfSHY JfTcft^ ^THT ^4t ^Tt^f ^r^trT^ ^^T^lrftfT^ According to the commentator we have first, 12 Rishis, who, as Rishis, are to be invoked, when the Brahmanical thread is suspended round the neck (nivita). These are indeed the Rishis of the Rig-veda : first the 6atarchins, the common title of the poets of the first Mandala ; then Gritsamada (2d Mandala"), Visvamitra (3d M.), Vamadeva (4th M.), Atri (5th M.), Bharadvaja (6th M.), Vasishtha (7th M.); then follow the poets of the Pra- MAHABHARATA. 43 Bharata, and according to some MSS. even the name of the Mahabharata, is mentioned, this may be considered as the earliest trace, not merely of single epic poems, but of a collection of them. The age of Asvalayana, which will be approximately fixed afterwards, would, therefore, if we can rely on our MSS., furnish a limit below which the first attempt at a collection of a Bharata or Mahabharata ought not to be placed. But there is no hope that we shall ever succeed by critical researches in restoring the Bharata to that primitive form and shape in which it may have existed before or at the time of Asvalayana. Much has indeed been done by Professor Lassen, who, in his Indian Antiquities, has pointed out characteristic marks by which the modern parts of the Mahabharata can be distinguished from the more ancient ; and we may soon expect to see his principles still farther carried out in a translation of the whole Mahabharata, which, with the help of all the Sanskrit commentaries, has been most carefully prepared by one of the most learned and laborious scholars of Germany. If it were possible to sift out from the huge mass of Indian epic poetry, as we now possess it in the Mahabharata and Ramayana, those old stories and songs gatha hymns (8th M.), the poets of the Pavamanis (9th M.), and finally, the authors of the 10th and last Mandala, who are called Kshudrasuktas and Mahasuktas, authors of short and long hymns. The next class comprises twenty-three invocations, according to the Commentary, and they are to be made, when the Brahmanical cord is suspended over the right shoulder (prachinaviti). The text, however, contains more than twenty-three names, and it is likely that some of them have been added afterwards, while others are perhaps to be taken collectively. ^U^ri^JTMl^K maJ a^s0 be taken as one word, in the sense of the legal authorities of the Bharatas.

which must have been' living for a long time in the mouth of the people before they were collected, enlarged, arranged, and dressed up by later hands, a rich mine of information would be opened for the ancient times of India, and very likely also for the Vedic age. But the whole frame of the two epic poems as they now stand, their language and metre, as well as the moral and religious system they contain, show that they were put together at a period when the world of the Veda was living by tradition only, and, moreover, partly misunderstood, and partly forgotten. The war between the Kurus and Pandavas, which forms the principal object of our Mahabharata, is unknown in the Veda. The names of the Kurus and Bharatas are common in the Vedic literature, but the names of the Pandavas have never been met with. It has been observed l, that even in Panini's grammar the name Pandu. or Pandava does not occur, while the Kurus and Bharatas are frequently mentioned, particularly in rules treating of the formation of patronymics and similar words.2 If, then, Asvalayana 1 Dr. Weber, Indische Studien, p. 148. Katyayana, however, the immediate successor of Panini, knows not only Pandu, but also his descendants, the Pandyas.

2 The names of the two wives of Pandu, Kunti and Madri, occur in the commentary on Panini. (Cf. i. 2. 49., iv. 1. 65., iv. 1. 176. (text) for Kunti, and iv. 1. 177. for Madri). But both these names are geographical appellatives, Kunti signifying a woman from the country of the Kuntas, Madri a Madra-woman. Prithd, another name of Kunti, stands in the Gana sivadi. As to the proper names of the Pandava princes, we find Yudhishthira, Pan. vi. 1. 134., vi. 3. 9., viii. 3. 95. (text); Arjuna, Pan. iii. 1. 119., iv. 3. 64., v. 4. 48., vi, 2. 131.; Bhima, Pan. vi. 1. 205.; Ndkula, Pan. vi. 3. 75. The name of Sahadeva does not occur ; but his descendants, the Sahadevas, are mentioned as belonging to the race of Kuru, together with the Nakulas, Pan. iv. 1. 114. In the same MAHABIIAKATA. i 45 can be shown to have been a contemporary, or at least an immediate successor, of Panini, the Bharata which he is speaking of must have been very different from the epic poem which is known to us way we find the descendants of Yudhishthira and Arjuna mentioned as members of the eastern Bharatas, Pan. ii. 4. 66. DraupadVs name does not occur in Panini, but Subhadra the sister of Krishna and the wife of Arjuna, is distinctly mentioned, Pan. iv. 2. 56. Another passage in the commentary on Panini (iv. 3. 87.) proves even the existence of a poem in praise of Subhadra, which, if we remember the former mention of a war about Subhadra (iv. 2. 56.), seems most likely to have celebrated this very conquest of Subhadra by Arjuna. In the Mahabharata this story forms a separate chapter, the Subhadra-harana-parva (Adiparva, p. 288.), which may be the very work which Panini, according to his commentator, is alluding to. That the chapter in the Mahabharata belongs to the oldest parts of this epic, may be seen from its being mentioned in the Anukramani of Dhritarashtra (i. 149.). " When I heard that Subhadra, of the race of Madhu, had been forcibly seized in the city of Dvaraka, and carried away by Arjuna, and that the two heroes of the race of Vrislmi had repaired to Indraprastha, I then, O Sanjaya, had no hope of success." The Mahabhashya, however, does not explain the former Sutra, (iv. 2. 56.), and for the latter it gives examples for the exceptions only, but not for the rule. The word grantha, used in the Sutra, (iv. 3. 87.), is always somewhat suspicious. That some of the Sutras which now form part of Panini 's grammar, did not proceed from him, is acknowledged by Kaiyyata, (cf. iv. 3. 131, 132.)

^Tf%^Tf^<*J 3 l M l fW^ft^f^T^ Tfa t"5re::il Krishna Vasudeva, who is considered as peculiarly connected with the tradition of the Pandavas, is quoted as Vasudeva, of the race of Vrishni (Pan. iv. 1. 114.); as Vasudeva, together with Siva and Aditya (Pan. v. 3. 99.) ; as Vasudeva, together with Arjuna (iv. 3. 98. text). In the commentary to Pan. iii. 3. 156., and ii. 3. 72., we have proof of Krishna's being worshipped as a god ; in i. 4. 92. he is mentioned as a hero. His residence, Dvaraka, however, does not occur in Panini.
zh: 目前討論那些在上述著作中用以確定佛教宗教起源的歷史年代之所有論據，將不甚恰當。佛陀宗教的年代：「地球人口數由哈塞爾先生評估為九億二千一百萬人；由馬爾特-布倫評估為六億四千二百萬人；由他人評估為七億三千七百萬人。佛教在幾乎整個中華帝國內信奉，該帝國單獨根據不同的計算，即包含一億八千四百萬至三億居民。再加上東方數個島嶼、交趾支那、暹羅、緬甸地區、印度、尼泊爾、西藏與大韃靼地區大部分的佛教徒等等，人們將會發現我對現今佛教徒人數的估計並未過度誇大。」
見 J. Bird，《佛教及耆那教起源與原理的歷史研究》（Historical Researches on the Origin and Principles of the Buddha and Jaina Religion），孟買，1847 年。

死亡時間，即西元前六世紀中葉，以及錫蘭紀年的開始（西元前543年），將在後文詳加考察。就目前而言，只需記住佛教紀年以同樣的方式劃分了印度的全部歷史為兩個部分，如同基督教紀年劃分了世界歷史一樣。因此，就吠陀文獻的歷史而言，這具有最大的重要性。一種新宗教的興起，對於婆羅門的等級制度如此敵對，最有可能在他們的聖典和神學著作中產生明顯的影響。如若能在印度的古代文獻中發現這類痕跡，將會獲得一個重要的突破點，並有可能為這龐大的婆羅門學術體系賦予某種歷史的關聯性。在佛陀之後的最初幾個世紀，新的宗教教義興起之後，不能期待婆羅門文獻會立即停止發展。相反地，我們應該預期首先會出現一種有力的反應，以及最後一次努力來抵抗這一新興教義的影響。而且，由於在印度佛教的宗教特別針對普通民眾階級，並在那些必須承受婆羅門體系排他性之苦的人中間獲得最強有力的支持，過渡時期最有可能以更加通俗的文學風格為特徵——是對舊有複雜的婆羅門儀式體系的一種簡化的嘗試，直到最後，通過被統治的王侯（如阿育王）所採納而為新教義所獲得的政治優勢，也會導致這種努力最終衰落。
在此之前，必須先說明這確實是真的 D 2 36 非吠陀著作。
既然宗教上的危機發生在印度，而且布拉門教吠陀文獻中存在著這場宗教危機的痕跡，那麼在探討吠陀時代的真實歷史證據時，就有必要指出哪些梵文著作可以被納入該文獻之中，以及哪些其他著作應當完全被排除在外。

讓我們先從反面的方法著手，努力區分並排除那些不屬於真正吠陀循環的著作。若我們審視印度的兩部史詩《羅摩衍那》和《摩訶婆羅多》，我們會發現不可能將它們作為吠陀時代的權威文獻，因為我們迄今尚無法批判性地判定這些詩篇的哪些部分是古代的，哪些是近代的、後佛教時期的，或至少是經後代編纂者與校訂者之手修飾過的。這兩部詩篇中確實包含許多非常古老的傳統與真正的吠陀時代傳說。它們的某些英雄人物取自吠陀詩歌所活動的同一史詩循環。然而，這些人物只是這兩部詩篇中插曲的題材，而它們的主要英雄人物在性格與習俗上本質上不同。實際上，雖然在史詩中確實可以發現吠陀時代的遺跡，如烏爾瓦希與普魯拉瓦斯、莎崑塔拉與度士曼多、烏陀羅迦、蘇那賽法、闍那迦雅及提舍的故事，特別是吠陀時代的聖者（如瓦西斯塔、毗濕瓦密多羅、雅吉那瓦爾基亞、迪爾伽塔瑪斯、卡克希瓦、卡瓦沙及許多其他聖者）的記載，然而這只能證明吠陀時代的傳統在印度史詩最初創作時仍在民間流傳，或在此後的年代中尚未被遺忘，當時婆羅門開始將所有史詩歌謠的遺存匯集成一部龐大的著作，稱為《摩訶婆羅多》。若我們將這些傳說在吠陀的讚歌與梵書中的呈現方式與《摩訶婆羅多》、《羅摩衍那》或《往世書》中的敍述相比較，吠陀時代版本的這些傳說多半會被發現比史詩與往世書詩篇中的版本更加簡樸、更加原始、更加容易理解。這並非否認在印度及其他雅利安民族中，真正的史詩——即慶頌諸神與英雄的力量與功績的大量民間歌謠——在極早期就已存在；但這表明，若其仍然存在，我們不應在《摩訶婆羅多》與《羅摩衍那》中去尋找這些古老的歌謠，而應在吠陀本身中去尋求。在吠陀讚歌集中，有些讚歌可以被稱為史詩性的，並可與據傳為荷馬所作的短讚歌相比擬。在梵書中，散見有散文與韻文段落，頌揚古代諸王的業績。
下列摘自《商迦耶那經》（Sankhayana-sutras）（xvi. 1.）的段落，對於婆羅門人所擁有的文獻投以某些光照，這些文獻乃是吾人習慣所稱的吠陀 * 之外的著作：——

在馬祭（Horse-sacrifice）中，阿特瓦里祭司（Adhvaryu）召喚歌手們，他們以琴樂伴奏而歌唱（vinaganaginas），並邀請他們讚頌執行祭祀的國王，連同其他古代的賢德之王。在祭祀的第一天，祭司講述一個以曼奴‧外外斯瓦塔（Manu Vaivasvata）開始的故事。因為曼奴的人民是人類，而在祭祀中亦有人類在場，祭司因此教導他們， --- 1 同樣的記載見於《阿修瓦拉亞那經文》（Asvalayana-sutras）第十卷第七節，以及《百路梵書》（Satapatha-brahmana）第十三卷第三章第一節第一段。
家主們，通過述說這個故事。他隨後說，「梨俱吠陀詩頌即是吠陀，這就是吠陀」，並吟誦一首讚歌。
在第二天，他講述了始於亞摩‧外瓦斯瓦塔（Yama Vaivasvata）的故事（取自《百道梵書》Satapatha）。由於亞摩的人民是祖先，而在場有父長存在，他透過這個故事教導長者。之後他說，「祭祀吠陀（Yajurveda）是吠陀；這就是吠陀」，並且誦唱《祭祀經》（Yajush）的一篇阿紐瓦卡（Anuvaka）（馬匹祭祀篇 asvamedhika）。
第三天，他講述了以伐樓那‧阿迪提亞（Varuna Aditya）開始的故事。因為伐樓那的人民是乾闥婆，且他們現場在座，他就以這則故事來教導年輕英俊的青年。然後他說：「阿闥婆吠陀就是吠陀；這就是吠陀」，並誦讀《藥人集》（Bhishaja），一部關於醫學的著作。
第四天，他講述一個開始於「蘇摩‧毘濕奴」（Soma Vaishnava，出自《百道梵書》）的故事。因為蘇摩的子民是阿普薩羅斯（Apsaras），而這些精靈確實在場，他藉由這個故事教導年輕貌美的少女。隨後他說：「『昂吉羅薩‧吠陀』是吠陀；這就是吠陀」，並誦讀《高羅經》（Ghora）——另一部阿闥婆尼卡派（Atharvanikas）的著作。

第五天，他講述始於阿爾布達‧卡德拉維亞（Arbuda Kadraveya）的故事。由於阿爾布達的人民是蛇族（Sarpas），而這些人現在就在場，他藉由這個故事教導蛇族或馴蛇者。隨後他說：「蛇明法（Sarpavidya）就是吠陀；這就是吠陀」，並誦唱蛇明法。 第六天，他講述始於庫貝拉‧毗沙羅婆那（Kuvera Vaisravana）的故事。由於庫貝拉的人民是羅剎（Rakshas），而這些人現在就在場，他藉由這個故事教導塞拉迦（Selagas）或作惡者。隨後他說：「羅剎明法（Rakshovidya）就是吠陀，這就是吠陀」，並誦唱羅剎明法。 第七天，他講述始於阿西塔‧丹瓦那（Asita Dhanvana）的故事。由於他的人民是阿修羅（Asuras），而這些人現在就在場，他藉由這個故事教導放債者（Kusidin）。隨後他說：「阿修羅明法（Asuravidya）就是吠陀，這就是吠陀」，並施展手法戲法。 第八天，他講述始於馬茲亞‧薩摩達（Matsya Sdmmada）的故事。由於他的人民是水生生物，而這些人現在就在場，他藉由這個故事教導馬茲亞（Matsyas，魚類）或漁夫。
第四部分 第四十頁

故事。隨後他說，「《伊帝訶娑吠陀》（Itihasa-veda）即是吠陀，此乃吠陀」，並誦唱一部《伊帝訶娑》。¹ 在第九天，他講述以《特爾克夏亞·瓦伊帕詩亞塔》（Tarkshya Vaipasyata）開篇的故事。² 由於他的人民是鳥類，而這些鳥類現已臨在，他以此故事教導鳥類，或年輕的學生（brahmacharin）³。隨後他說，「《普拉那吠陀》（Purana-veda）即是吠陀，此乃吠陀」，並誦唱《普拉那》。⁴ 在第十天，他講述以《法·因陀羅》（Dharma Indra，出自《百路梵書》）開篇的故事。由於他的人民是眾神，而這些眾神現已臨在，他以此故事教導年輕、博學且貧困的祭司。⁵ 隨後他說，「《娑摩吠陀》（Samaveda）即是吠陀，此乃吠陀」，並唱誦《娑摩歌》（Sama）。⁶
此段節錄表明，在吠陀時代既存在著傳統的史詩詩歌，亦存在著即興創作的史詩詩歌。
在《梵書》（Brahmanas）和《森林書》（Aranyakas）的若干篇章中，當敘述古代印度人所知的文獻時，我們會遇到「歌謠」（Gatha）、「英雄傳說」（Narasansi）、「史詩」（Itihasa）和「故事」（Akhyana）7 這些名稱，它們是文獻的組成部分 2 《博學者》（Vaipaschita），依據阿濕瓦拉亞納（Asvalayana）所言。

41 吠陀文獻中。這類文學作品標題的出現，曾被用來證明該早期時代確實存在後來以相同名稱指稱的著作。但儘管《摩訶婆羅多》被稱為史詩（Itihasa），《羅摩衍那》被稱為敘事詩（Akhyana），儘管許多著作在後世以《往世書》（Puranas）之名而聞名，然而《梵書》中這些文學作品的列舉並不涉及它們。1 它們只含有通稱或 WT«Trf% ^TT*sM I lU^ ll ^id. iv. 1, 2., iv. 5. 9.; Satapath. Brahm.

Sanhita, xv. 6.: ^f^T^nf ^TTW "^ TTSTT^f •TnC^I^t'^' II 參見 Aufrecht, *Indische Studien*, p. 133。沙耶那本人有時也有疑問，例如在其《泰提利耶‧阿蘭亞卡》（Taittiriya-aranyaka）的注疏中，他說「往世書」（purana）可能是指《梵卵經》（Brahmanda）等；「史詩」（itihasa）則是指《摩訶婆羅多》（Mahabharata）。然而這是一個錯誤，這會使沙耶那與自己相互矛盾。他已在《梨俱吠陀》的導論中充分證明，在《泰提利耶‧阿蘭亞卡》的這段文中，不可能指的是與吠陀分離的其他著作。參見 *Rig-veda sanhita*, p. 23。韋伯博士在其《帕尼尼語法書》（Panini）摘錄中（iv. 2. 60.）指出，vyakhyana（解釋）、akhyana（敘述）、katha（故事）、akhyayika（傳奇）、itihasa（史詩）和 purana（往世書），都是迦底耶耶那（Katyayana）時代所知的文學著作的名稱。但他傾向於認為，迦底耶耶那用這些通稱所指的並非我們現在所擁有的《摩訶婆羅多》、《羅摩衍那》和往世書。參見 *Indische Studien*, i. p. 147。

在後世文獻中，《往世書》（Itihasa）、《故事》（Akhyana）與《往事書》（Purana）等名稱絕非侷限於《摩訶婆羅多》、《羅摩衍那》與《往事書》。《摩訶婆羅多》亦被稱為《往事書》、《故事》與《往世書》。參見《摩訶婆羅多》第一卷第17至19頌。毗耶娑自己稱其詩作《摩訶婆羅多》為詩文（Kavya），且梵天以此為其正式名號。參見《摩訶婆羅多》第一卷第72頌。此段落修正了拉森教授認為詩文（Kavya）為《羅摩衍那》特有標題的意見。參見《印度古物志》第一卷第480頁。《摩訶婆羅多》共四十二部史詩。

標題，這些標題被應用於聖典的某些部分，包含神祇或人物的故事，或宇宙起源傳統。¹ 在吠陀文獻中，無論是婆羅門書或經典，都沒有提及任何往世書或羅摩衍那的標題。但如在阿什瓦拉衍那（Asvalayana）的經典中，該部典籍也被稱為第五吠陀，或克里希納吠陀；即由克里希納·德瓦伊帕衍那·毘耶娑（Krishna Dvaipayana Vyasa）所編撰的吠陀。參見《摩訶婆羅多》第一卷第 2300 行。Burnouf，《薄伽梵歌》第三卷，序言第 xxi 頁。Lassen，《印度古代》第一卷第 789 頁。
參見沙耶納著《梨俱吠陀‧本集》導論，第 23 頁。

2 格里亥亞蘇特拉（Grihy a-Sutras），卷三，章四。手稿 1978，東方研究所（E. I. H.）作 *TR7T- ^T^T^r:，而羅特博士（Zur Literatur，頁27）所採讀本為 ^TT^TWTTrfWr^T^r:。不幸的是，此段落的注疏極為簡略，更令人遺憾的是，經文本身似乎含有偽造的增補。根據手稿記載，此段落讀作：^ro ^ra: ipTf^r TTSTW *T<^f>* f^TfSHY ■JfTcft^ ^THT ^4t ^Tt^f ^r^trT^ ^^T^lrftfT^ 據注家所言，首先有十二位仙人（Rishis），作為仙人，當梵學子弦懸於頸部（nivita）時應被祈求。這些確實是梨俱吠陀的仙人：首先是六位詩人家族首領（atarchins），即第一卷（Mandala）詩人的通稱；其次是格里特薩瑪達（第二卷）、毗濕瓦密多羅（第三卷）、瓦瑪德瓦（第四卷）、阿特里（第五卷）、跋囉德瓦闍（第六卷）、瓦西什塔（第七卷）；隨後是《原梵經》（Pra-Gatha）讚歌的詩人們（第八卷）、淨化讚歌（Pavamanis）的詩人們（第九卷），最後是第十卷及最後一卷的作者，稱為短讚歌與長讚歌的作者（Kshudrasuktas and Mahasuktas）。下一類包括根據注家所言的二十三個祈求，當梵學子弦懸於右肩（prachinaviti）時應進行。然而經文包含超過二十三個名號，很可能其中一些是後來添加的，而另一些也許應作為整體看待。 **摩訶婆羅多。** 若摩訶婆羅多的名稱被提及，這可被視為非僅單一史詩詩歌，而是其集合的最早痕跡。阿斯瓦拉亞那（Asvalayana）的時代，其將在後文被大致確定，因此若我們可信賴我們的手稿，將提供一個下限，在此之下不應將摩訶婆羅多或摩訶婆羅多集合的首次嘗試置於此下。但我們永遠沒有希望通過批判性研究成功地將摩訶婆羅多復原至其在阿斯瓦拉亞那時代之前或時代的原始形態與樣貌。確實，拉森教授已做了許多工作，他在其《印度古物志》中指出了特徵性標記，藉此可將摩訶婆羅多的現代部分與更古老的部分區分開來；我們很快將期待看到他的原則在一部完整摩訶婆羅多的譯本中進一步推展，該譯本在所有梵文注家的幫助下，已由德國最博學與最勤奮的學者之一精心準備。若有可能從我們現今在摩訶婆羅多與羅摩衍那中所擁有的龐大印度史詩詩歌堆積中篩選出那些古老的故事與歌曲，

這些詩歌必定在人民口中流傳了很長一段時間，才被後人蒐集、擴充、編排並加工潤飾。若能詳加考究，將為印度古代開啟豐富的訊息寶藏，很可能也能為吠陀時代提供資料。然而，兩部史詩以現存形式所呈現的整體框架、其語言與韻律，以及所包含的道德與宗教體系，都表明它們是在吠陀世界已經僅存於傳統之中時期才編纂而成的；而且不僅如此，其內容還有一部分被誤解，一部分則已被遺忘。構成我們《摩訶婆羅多》主要內容的俱盧與般度之戰，在吠陀中完全不見蹤跡。俱盧人與婆羅多人的名字在吠陀文獻中甚為常見，但般度人的名字則從未出現過。有人指出¹，即使在帕尼尼的語法書中，般度（Pandu）或般度人（Pandava）這個名字也未曾出現，而俱盧人與婆羅多人卻經常被提及，特別是在討論族名衍生詞及類似用語形成的規則中。² 若阿斯瓦拉亞那 ¹ Weber 博士，《印度研究》，第 148 頁。然而，帕尼尼的直接後繼者迦坦亞那（Katyayana）不僅知道般度（Pandu），也知道他的後代——般度耶人（Pandyas）。

2 般若尼（Pandu）的兩位妻子昆提（Kunti）和瑪德麗（Madri）的名字出現在帕尼尼（Panini）的註疏中。（參見 i. 2. 49.、iv. 1. 65.、iv. 1. 176.（本文）涉及昆提，以及 iv. 1. 177. 涉及瑪德麗）。但這兩個名字都是地理性的通稱詞，昆提意為來自昆塔（Kuntas）國家的女性，瑪德麗為瑪德拉（Madra）女性。普麗莎（Prithá），昆提的另一個名字，出現在《西瓦地集》（Gana sivadi）中。至於般度五子（Pandava）王子們的專有名字，我們發現尤迪什蒂拉（Yudhishthira），帕尼尼 vi. 1. 134.、vi. 3. 9.、viii. 3. 95.（本文）；阿周那（Arjuna），帕尼尼 iii. 1. 119.、iv. 3. 64.、v. 4. 48.、vi. 2. 131.；毗摩（Bhima），帕尼尼 vi. 1. 205.；那古羅（Nâkula），帕尼尼 vi. 3. 75.。薩哈德瓦（Sahadeva）的名字並未出現；但他的後代薩哈德瓦族（Sahadevas）被提及為屬於俱盧（Kuru）種族，與那古羅族（Nakulas）一起，帕尼尼 iv. 1. 114.。同樣的方式，我們發現尤迪什蒂拉和阿周那的後代被提及為東方婆羅多族（eastern Bharatas）的成員，帕尼尼 ii. 4. 66.。德勞帕蒂（Draupadî）的名字在帕尼尼中並未出現，但蘇跋德羅（Subhadra），克里希納（Krishna）的妹妹和阿周那的妻子，在帕尼尼 iv. 2. 56. 中被明確提及。帕尼尼註疏中的另一段文字（iv. 3. 87.）甚至證實了一首讚頌蘇跋德羅的詩歌存在，若我們想起前面關於蘇跋德羅之戰的提及（iv. 2. 56.），這很可能是慶祝阿周那對蘇跋德羅的征服。在《摩訶婆羅多》中，這個故事構成了一個獨立的篇章——《蘇跋德羅劫奪篇》（Subhadra-harana-parva）（《初篇》，第 288 頁），這很可能就是帕尼尼按照其註疏者之說所指涉的著作。《摩訶婆羅多》中的這個篇章屬於這部史詩最古老的部分，可從其在持國王（Dhritarashtra）的《索引》（Anukramani）（i. 149.）中被提及看出。「當我聽聞摩度（Madhu）種族的蘇跋德羅在德瓦卡（Dvaraka）城被強行掠奪，並被阿周那帶走，而毗利希尼（Vrishni）種族的兩位英雄已經前往因陀羅城（Indraprastha）時，桑傑亞（Sanjaya）啊，我就不抱成功的希望了。」然而，《摩訶婆什亞》（Mahabhashya）對前面的蘇特拉（Sutra）（iv. 2. 56.）並未作出解釋，對於後者，它只給出例外的例子，而非對該規則的解釋。蘇特拉（iv. 3. 87.）中使用的「經文」（grantha）一詞總是相當可疑的。現在構成帕尼尼文法一部分的某些蘇特拉（Sutra）並非源自他本人，這一點已為迦耶塔（Kaiyyata）所承認（參見 iv. 3. 131、132.）。

克里希納‧瓦蘇德瓦（Krishna Vasudeva）被認為與般度族（Pandavas）的傳統特別相關，在文獻中被引述為毗利奴族（race of Vrishni）的瓦蘇德瓦（Pan. iv. 1. 114.）；同時與濕婆和阿迪提亞（Aditya）並列的瓦蘇德瓦（Pan. v. 3. 99.）；以及與阿周那並列的瓦蘇德瓦（iv. 3. 98. text）。在帕尼尼文法書第三卷第三章第156節及第二卷第三章第72節的注疏中，我們有證據顯示克里希納被視為一位神祇；在第一卷第四章第92節中，他被提及為一位英雄。然而，他的住所德瓦拉卡（Dvaraka）在帕尼尼的著作中並未出現。
